text: My name is Mark Glover from Austin, Texas.
This is a video of a robotic unoscolar hernia that was present for approximately seven to eight years prior to surgery.
The video shows the ascending and transverse colon, along with a significant portion of omentum, visibly incarcerated within the hernia sac.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic reduction of the hernia contents, preparing for robotic docking.
The surgeon performs simultaneous laparoscopic manipulation while manually manipulating the scrotum at the bedside, coordinating both approaches for effective hernia reduction.
This also allows haptic feedback of how much pressure and tension you're putting on the bowel.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the bowel without directly grasping it, using techniques to minimize manipulation and potential trauma during the robotic hernia repair.
The surgeon uses the shaft of the instrument to apply lateral pressure on the bowel without directly grasping it, facilitating manipulation within the hernia repair site.
Reducing that amount of chronically incarcerated bowel can be time consuming, and this took about forty-five minutes to reduce the bowel completely.
After completely reducing the bowel, the surgical team docks the robotic system and initiates preperitoneal dissection, preparing the surgical field for hernia repair.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac completely, using either laparoscopic or robotic techniques, ensuring the sac is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
However, in this individual, that is not feasible.
The surgeon isolates the peritoneal sac from the cord structures, visibly separating these anatomical components during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon transects the peritoneal sac after completing distal dissection.
These large immunoscoridal hernias often develop a seroma
and the patient is informed of this prior to surgery.
The video shows the peritoneal defect left after the hernia sac has been transected, with the surrounding tissue clearly visible.
The surgeon selects a larger heavyweight polypropylene mesh to repair the large peritoneal defect, following the reduction of a significant amount of bowel contents back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon transfixes the heavyweight polypropylene mesh over the peritoneal defect, securing it in place to cover the area where the hernia sac was previously transected.
The surgeon secures the mesh in multiple locations using absorbable sutures, ensuring stable fixation within the surgical field.
The surgeon fixates the mesh over the hernia defect and begins closing the peritoneum, using sutures to secure the mesh in place.
The surgeon completes the peritoneal closure, effectively closing the defect and ensuring the mesh is fully excluded from the peritoneal cavity.
The patient was discharged home the same day, returned to work within two weeks.
